title: Risk and Return Fundamentals
description: Define and calculate various measures of investment return, distinguish between systematic and unsystematic risk, calculate standard deviation and coefficient of variation, and apply the risk-return tradeoff in investment decisions.
topics: Standard deviation and coefficient of variation

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: 🧠 Understanding the relationship between risk and return is the foundation of all investment planning.

sections.title: Measures of Return
sections.content:
### Holding Period Return (HPR)

Total return over a specific time period.

$$\text{HPR} = \frac{\text{Ending Value} - \text{Beginning Value} + \text{Income}}{\text{Beginning Value}}$$

sections.title: HPR Example
sections.content:
📊 **Calculating Holding Period Return**

| Item | Value |
|------|-------|
| Purchase price | $100 |
| Sale price | $115 |
| Dividends received | $3 |

$$\text{HPR} = \frac{\$115 - \$100 + \$3}{\$100} = 18\%$$

sections.title: Annualized Returns
sections.content:
### Arithmetic Mean Return

Simple average of periodic returns.

$$\bar{R} = \frac{R_1 + R_2 + ... + R_n}{n}$$

**Use**: Expected return calculations, comparing strategies.

### Geometric Mean Return (CAGR)

Compound annual growth rate—actual growth rate over time.

$$\text{Geometric Mean} = \left[(1+R_1)(1+R_2)...(1+R_n)\right]^{1/n} - 1$$

**Use**: Actual historical performance, time-weighted returns.

sections.title: Memory Aid
sections.content: 🧠 Arithmetic mean is always ≥ geometric mean (except when all returns are equal). **Geometric = Get the actual Growth**

sections.title: Arithmetic vs. Geometric Example
sections.content:
📊 **Two-Year Return Comparison**

| Year | Return | Value ($1,000 start) |
|------|--------|---------------------|
| 1 | +50% | $1,500 |
| 2 | -50% | $750 |

**Arithmetic mean**: (50% + -50%) / 2 = **0%**

**Geometric mean**: √(1.50 × 0.50) - 1 = √0.75 - 1 = **-13.4%**

The geometric mean reflects reality—you lost money!

sections.title: Real vs. Nominal Returns
sections.content:
### Fisher Equation

$$\text{Real Return} ≈ \text{Nominal Return} - \text{Inflation Rate}$$

More precisely:

$$1 + R_{\text{real}} = \frac{1 + R_{\text{nominal}}}{1 + \text{Inflation}}$$

sections.title: Real Return Example
sections.content:
📊 **Calculating Real Return**

| Item | Rate |
|------|------|
| Nominal return | 8% |
| Inflation | 3% |

$$R_{\text{real}} = \frac{1.08}{1.03} - 1 = 4.85\%$$

sections.title: Types of Investment Risk
sections.content:
### Systematic Risk (Market Risk)

**Cannot be diversified away.** Affects entire market.

sections.title: Unsystematic Risk Types
sections.content:
### Unsystematic Risk (Diversifiable Risk)

**Can be diversified away.** Specific to company/industry.

sections.title: Memory Aid: Systematic Risks
sections.content: 🧠 **PRIME** risks are systematic: **P**urchasing power, **R**einvestment, **I**nterest rate, **M**arket, **E**xchange rate

sections.title: Measuring Risk: Standard Deviation
sections.content:
### Definition

Measures dispersion of returns around the mean.

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum(R_i - \bar{R})^2}{n-1}}$$

sections.title: Normal Distribution Rule
sections.content:
- 68% of returns within ±1σ
- 95% of returns within ±2σ
- 99% of returns within ±3σ

sections.title: Coefficient of Variation (CV)
sections.content:
Measures risk per unit of return.

$$\text{CV} = \frac{\sigma}{\bar{R}}$$

**When to Use**: Comparing investments with different expected returns.

sections.title: CV Comparison Example
sections.content:
📊 **Comparing Risk-Adjusted Returns**

| Investment | Return | Std Dev | CV |
|------------|--------|---------|-----|
| A | 10% | 15% | 1.50 |
| B | 20% | 25% | 1.25 |

**Investment B** has better risk-adjusted return (lower CV).

sections.title: Risk-Return Tradeoff
sections.content:
### The Fundamental Principle

Higher expected returns require accepting higher risk.

sections.title: Implications for Planning
sections.content:
- **Young clients**: Can accept more risk (longer time horizon)
- **Retirees**: Generally need lower risk
- **Risk capacity ≠ Risk tolerance**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: **Geometric mean** reflects actual performance; arithmetic mean for forecasting
**Systematic risk** cannot be diversified; unsystematic risk can be
**Standard deviation** measures total volatility of returns
**Coefficient of variation** = risk per unit of return (lower is better)
**Higher returns require higher risk**—no free lunch in investing

sections.title: Key Formulas
sections.content:
- **HPR**: (End - Begin + Income) / Begin
- **Geometric Mean**: [(1+R₁)(1+R₂)...(1+Rₙ)]^(1/n) - 1
- **Real Return**: ≈ Nominal Return - Inflation
- **CV**: Standard Deviation / Expected Return

knowledgeCheck.question: An investor purchased a stock for $50 at the beginning of the year. During the year, they received $2 in dividends and the stock price increased to $55 by the end of the year. What is the Holding Period Return (HPR)?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The HPR is calculated as (Ending Value - Beginning Value + Income) / Beginning Value. In this case, ($55 - $50 + $2) / $50 = $7 / $50 = 0.14 or 14%.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to include all income received during the holding period in your calculation.

rows.label: Usefulness
values: Estimating expected returns in the short term, comparing investment strategies.
Measuring actual performance over a period, especially useful for investments with fluctuating returns.

rows.label: Impact of Volatility
values: Not affected by the sequence of returns.
Sensitive to the volatility of returns; lower than arithmetic mean when returns are highly variable.

reveal.question: What type of return is considered the best measure of past performance of an investment?
reveal.answer: Geometric Mean Return, because it reflects the actual compound rate of return earned over the investment period, taking into account the effects of compounding.

title: Modern Portfolio Theory
description: Explain the benefits of diversification, calculate portfolio expected return and standard deviation, interpret correlation and its impact on portfolio risk, and identify the efficient frontier and optimal portfolios.

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: 🧠 **Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT)**, developed by Harry Markowitz (1952), revolutionized investing by showing how to optimize the risk-return tradeoff through diversification.

sections.title: The Power of Diversification
sections.content:
### Key Insight

Combining assets with less-than-perfect correlation reduces portfolio risk **without sacrificing return**.

### Mathematical Foundation

Two assets with same return and same risk:
- Perfect correlation (+1): No risk reduction
- Zero correlation (0): Some risk reduction
- Negative correlation (-1): Maximum risk reduction

sections.title: Portfolio Expected Return
sections.content:
The weighted average of individual expected returns.

$$E(R_p) = w_1 E(R_1) + w_2 E(R_2) + ... + w_n E(R_n)$$

sections.title: Two-Asset Portfolio Example
sections.content:
📊 **Calculating Portfolio Expected Return**

| Asset | Weight | Expected Return |
|-------|--------|-----------------|
| Stocks | 60% | 10% |
| Bonds | 40% | 5% |

$$E(R_p) = 0.60(10\%) + 0.40(5\%) = 8\%$$

sections.title: Portfolio Risk (Standard Deviation)
sections.content:
### Two-Asset Formula

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{w_1^2\sigma_1^2 + w_2^2\sigma_2^2 + 2w_1w_2\sigma_1\sigma_2\rho_{1,2}}$$

Where:
- $w$ = weight
- $\sigma$ = standard deviation
- $\rho$ = correlation coefficient

sections.title: Portfolio Risk Depends On
sections.content:
🧠 Portfolio risk depends on:
1. Individual asset risks (σ)
2. Weights (w)
3. **Correlation (ρ)** between assets

sections.title: Correlation Coefficient (ρ)
sections.content: ### Range and Interpretation

sections.title: Correlation Impact Example
sections.content:
📊 **Portfolio Risk at Different Correlations**

| Given | Value |
|-------|-------|
| Stock σ | 20% |
| Bond σ | 8% |
| Stock weight | 60% |
| Bond weight | 40% |

**If ρ = +1.0 (Perfect Positive)**
$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{0.36(0.04) + 0.16(0.0064) + 2(0.60)(0.40)(0.20)(0.08)(1.0)}$$
$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{0.0144 + 0.001024 + 0.00768} = \sqrt{0.023104} = 15.2\%$$

**If ρ = 0 (No Correlation)**
$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{0.0144 + 0.001024 + 0} = \sqrt{0.015424} = 12.4\%$$

**If ρ = -1.0 (Perfect Negative)**
$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{0.0144 + 0.001024 - 0.00768} = \sqrt{0.007744} = 8.8\%$$

**Lower correlation → Lower portfolio risk!**

sections.title: Memory Aid
sections.content:
🧠 **MPT = Mix Properly Together** (for diversification)

**Correlation +1 = Copied movement; -1 = Contrary movement**

sections.title: The Efficient Frontier
sections.content:
### Definition

The set of portfolios that offer the **highest expected return for each level of risk**.

sections.title: Efficient Frontier Characteristics
rows: Optimal—cannot get more return without more risk

sections.title: The Curve
sections.content:
Moving along the efficient frontier:
- Left (lower risk, lower return)
- Right (higher risk, higher return)

sections.title: Optimal Portfolio Selection
sections.content:
### Combining with Risk-Free Asset

When you add a risk-free asset, the efficient frontier becomes a straight line called the **Capital Market Line (CML)**.

### Tangent Portfolio

The point where CML touches the efficient frontier:
- Called the **market portfolio**
- Contains all risky assets in market-value weights
- Maximum Sharpe ratio

sections.title: Assumptions of MPT
sections.content:
### The Model Assumes

1. Investors are rational and risk-averse
2. Returns are normally distributed
3. Investors choose portfolios based on mean and variance only
4. Transaction costs and taxes are zero
5. All investors have same time horizon
6. Investors have homogeneous expectations

sections.title: Exam Trap: MPT Limitations
sections.content:
⚠️ **Limitations of MPT**:
- Returns aren't always normally distributed (fat tails)
- Correlations change during crises (tend toward +1)
- Historical data may not predict future
- Ignores behavioral biases

sections.title: Practical Applications
sections.content:
### Asset Allocation

- Mix asset classes with low correlations
- International diversification
- Alternative investments (real estate, commodities)

### Rebalancing

- Maintain target weights
- Buy low, sell high automatically
- Control portfolio drift

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: **Diversification** reduces risk when correlation < +1
**Portfolio return** = weighted average of asset returns
**Portfolio risk** depends on weights, individual risks, AND correlations
**Lower correlation** = greater diversification benefit
**Efficient frontier**: Maximum return for given risk level

sections.title: Key Formulas
sections.content:
- **Portfolio Return**: E(Rp) = w₁E(R₁) + w₂E(R₂)
- **Portfolio Risk**: σp = √[w₁²σ₁² + w₂²σ₂² + 2w₁w₂σ₁σ₂ρ₁,₂]
- **Correlation Range**: -1 to +1

knowledgeCheck.question: An investor holds two assets in their portfolio. Asset A has an expected return of 10% and a standard deviation of 15%. Asset B has an expected return of 10% and a standard deviation of 15%. If the correlation coefficient between Asset A and Asset B is +1.0, what is the likely impact of adding Asset B to a portfolio that initially consisted only of Asset A?
options: The portfolio's expected return will increase, and the portfolio's standard deviation will decrease.
The portfolio's expected return will remain the same, and the portfolio's standard deviation will remain the same.
The portfolio's expected return will decrease, and the portfolio's standard deviation will increase.
The portfolio's expected return will increase, and the portfolio's standard deviation will increase.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: When the correlation coefficient is +1.0 (perfectly positively correlated), there is no diversification benefit. Adding Asset B will not change the overall risk (standard deviation) or expected return of the portfolio. Both assets have the same risk and return.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider what happens when assets move in perfect lockstep.

reveal.question: State the two-asset formula for portfolio standard deviation and define each variable.
reveal.answer:
σp = √(w1²σ1² + w2²σ2² + 2w1w2σ1σ2ρ1,2)

Where:
- w1 and w2 = weight of asset 1 and asset 2, respectively
- σ1 and σ2 = standard deviation of asset 1 and asset 2, respectively
- ρ1,2 = correlation coefficient between asset 1 and asset 2

knowledgeCheck.question: A portfolio consists of the following assets: 60% invested in Stock X with an expected return of 12%, and 40% invested in Bond Y with an expected return of 5%. What is the expected return of the portfolio?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The portfolio's expected return is calculated as follows: E(Rp) = (0.60 * 12%) + (0.40 * 5%) = 7.2% + 2.0% = 9.2%
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to weight the expected returns by their respective portfolio allocation.

title: Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)
description: Calculate expected return using CAPM, interpret beta and its implications, distinguish systematic from total risk, and apply the Security Market Line concept.

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: 🧠 The **CAPM** determines the expected return of an asset based on its systematic risk (beta), not total risk.

sections.title: The CAPM Formula
sections.content:
$$E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_m) - R_f]$$

Where:
- $E(R_i)$ = Expected return of the investment
- $R_f$ = Risk-free rate
- $\beta_i$ = Beta of the investment
- $E(R_m)$ = Expected return of the market
- $[E(R_m) - R_f]$ = Market risk premium

sections.title: Understanding Beta (β)
sections.content:
### Definition

Beta measures sensitivity to market movements—**systematic risk**.

$$\beta = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)}$$

sections.title: Portfolio Beta
sections.content:
$$\beta_p = w_1\beta_1 + w_2\beta_2 + ... + w_n\beta_n$$

Weighted average of individual betas.

sections.title: The Security Market Line (SML)
sections.content:
### Definition

A graph of CAPM showing the linear relationship between beta and expected return.

sections.title: Alpha (α)
sections.content:
### Definition

The excess return above what CAPM predicts.

$$\alpha = \text{Actual Return} - \text{Expected Return (from CAPM)}$$

sections.title: Alpha Example
sections.content:
📊 **Calculating Alpha**

Stock returned 14%, CAPM predicted 12.1%:
$$\alpha = 14\% - 12.1\% = +1.9\%$$

Positive alpha indicates good stock selection or timing.

sections.title: Memory Aid
sections.content:
🧠 **CAPM = Consider All Parts of the Market**

**Beta = Bounce with the market** (1=same, >1=more, <1=less)

sections.title: CAPM Key Insight
sections.content:
🧠 The market only rewards **systematic risk**:
- Unsystematic risk can be diversified away (free)
- Only systematic risk earns risk premium

sections.title: CAPM Assumptions
sections.content:
1. Investors are rational and risk-averse
2. All investors have same time horizon
3. Can borrow/lend at risk-free rate
4. No transaction costs or taxes
5. All information is available to everyone
6. Assets are infinitely divisible

sections.title: Exam Trap: CAPM Limitations
sections.content:
⚠️ **CAPM Limitations**:
- Beta is based on historical data
- Risk-free rate assumption is imperfect
- Market portfolio is theoretical (cannot truly own all assets)
- Single-factor model may be too simple

sections.title: Applications in Financial Planning
sections.content:
### Portfolio Construction
- Use beta to target desired market sensitivity
- Combine high/low beta for desired risk level

### Performance Evaluation
- Compare returns to CAPM expectations
- Identify managers with positive alpha

### Asset Allocation
- Adjust beta through allocation changes
- Use leverage to increase beta if needed

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: **CAPM**: E(R) = Rf + β(Rm - Rf)
**Beta** measures systematic risk (sensitivity to market)
**SML** graphs the risk-return relationship; above SML = undervalued
**Alpha** = actual return minus CAPM-expected return
**Only systematic risk** is rewarded—diversify away the rest

sections.title: Key Formulas
sections.content:
- **CAPM**: E(Ri) = Rf + βi(Rm - Rf)
- **Portfolio β**: w₁β₁ + w₂β₂ + ... + wₙβₙ
- **Alpha**: Actual Return - Expected Return
- **Market Risk Premium**: Rm - Rf

knowledgeCheck.question: An investment has a beta of 1.2. The risk-free rate is 2% and the expected market return is 10%. According to the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM), what is the expected return of the investment?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The CAPM formula is E(Ri) = Rf + βi [E(Rm) - Rf]. Plugging in the values, we get E(Ri) = 2% + 1.2 * (10% - 2%) = 2% + 1.2 * 8% = 2% + 9.6% = 11.6%.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to calculate the market risk premium first before multiplying by beta.

reveal.question: What does a beta of less than 1 indicate about an investment's volatility compared to the market?
reveal.answer: A beta less than 1 indicates that the investment is less volatile than the market. It means the investment's price tends to move less than the market as a whole. For example, a beta of 0.7 suggests that for every 1% change in the market, the investment's price is expected to change by 0.7% in the same direction.

rows.label: Definition
values: Risk inherent to the entire market or market segment, such as interest rate changes or recessions.
The overall risk of an investment, encompassing both systematic and unsystematic risk.

rows.label: CAPM Relevance
values: The CAPM focuses on systematic risk, as it is the only risk for which investors are compensated.
Total risk is not directly used in the CAPM.

title: Market Efficiency and Behavioral Finance
description: Explain the three forms of market efficiency, identify investment strategies implied by each form, recognize common behavioral biases, and apply behavioral insights to financial planning.

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: 🧠 The **Efficient Market Hypothesis (EMH)** suggests prices reflect available information. **Behavioral finance** challenges this by exploring psychological biases.

sections.title: Efficient Market Hypothesis (EMH)
sections.content:
### Core Premise

Stock prices fully reflect all available information, making it impossible to consistently "beat the market."

sections.title: Memory Aid: EMH Levels
sections.content: 🧠 **WeSH = Weak, Semi-strong, Strong** = all Hearing everything progressively

sections.title: Evidence for EMH
sections.content:
### Support for EMH

- Most active managers underperform indexes
- Information is incorporated quickly
- Past returns don't predict future returns reliably

sections.title: Implications for Financial Planning
sections.content:
### If You Believe in EMH

- Use **index funds** (low-cost, market exposure)
- Focus on **asset allocation** not stock picking
- Minimize **costs and taxes**
- Accept market returns

### If You Believe in Inefficiency

- Active management may add value
- Security selection matters
- Factor investing (value, momentum) may work
- Market timing may be possible

sections.title: Behavioral Finance
sections.content:
### Key Premise

Investors are not always rational—psychological biases affect decisions.

sections.title: Major Cognitive Biases
sections.content:
- **Anchoring**: Relying too heavily on first piece of information. Example: 'I bought at $50, so I won't sell until it gets back to $50.'
- **Confirmation Bias**: Seeking information that confirms existing beliefs. Example: Only reading positive news about stocks you own.
- **Mental Accounting**: Treating money differently based on its source. Example: Willing to gamble with 'house money' (gains) but not savings.
- **Overconfidence**: Overestimating one's own abilities. Example: Believing you can consistently beat the market.
- **Availability Bias**: Overweighting easily recalled information (recent or dramatic). Example: Fearing stocks after a crash, even if historically optimal.
- **Recency Bias**: Overweighting recent experience. Example: Expecting recent trends to continue indefinitely.

sections.title: Memory Aid: Cognitive Biases
sections.content: 🧠 **ALARM** biases: **A**nchoring, **L**oss aversion, **A**vailability, **R**ecency, **M**ental accounting

sections.title: Major Emotional Biases
sections.content:
- **Loss Aversion**: Losses hurt more than equivalent gains feel good. Research shows losses hurt about **2x** as much as gains please. Impact: Hold losers too long, sell winners too soon.
- **Endowment Effect**: Overvaluing things you already own. Example: Inherited stock feels more valuable than it is.
- **Herding**: Following the crowd. Example: Buying at market tops, selling at bottoms.
- **Regret Aversion**: Fear of making decisions that might be wrong. Example: Paralysis in making investment changes.

sections.title: Applying Behavioral Insights
rows: Frame in terms of goals, not losses
Focus on fundamentals, not purchase price

sections.title: Check-In Questions for Clients
sections.content:
💡 **Helpful questions to ask clients**:
- "Are you holding this because you believe in it—or because you bought it?"
- "Would you buy this today at current price?"

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: **EMH forms**: Weak, Semi-strong, Strong—progressively more information reflected
**Weak form**: Technical analysis fails; **Semi-strong**: Fundamental analysis fails
**Anomalies** (January effect, small-firm) challenge EMH
**Behavioral biases**: Loss aversion, overconfidence, herding, anchoring
**Planners** should recognize biases and design guardrails

sections.title: Key Concepts
sections.content:
- **EMH**: Efficient Market Hypothesis—no formulas, conceptual understanding of market theory
- **Behavioral Finance**: Study of psychological biases affecting investment decisions

knowledgeCheck.question: According to the Efficient Market Hypothesis (EMH), which of the following statements is MOST accurate regarding the ability to consistently outperform the market?
options: It is possible to consistently outperform the market by utilizing technical analysis.
It is possible to consistently outperform the market by utilizing insider information.
It is impossible to consistently outperform the market due to stock prices reflecting all available information.
It is possible to consistently outperform the market by utilizing fundamental analysis.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The EMH posits that stock prices already incorporate all available information, making it impossible for investors to consistently achieve returns above the average market return. While insider information might lead to short-term gains, it's illegal and unethical. Technical and fundamental analysis are unlikely to provide a consistent advantage.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the core premise of the EMH: information availability.

rows.label: Information Reflected
values: All publicly available information (including historical data)

reveal.question: What is the primary difference between cognitive and emotional biases?
reveal.answer: Cognitive biases arise from errors in information processing or memory, while emotional biases stem from feelings or emotions influencing decisions. Cognitive biases can potentially be corrected with better information and education, whereas emotional biases are more deeply rooted and harder to eliminate.

knowledgeCheck.question: A client consistently holds onto losing stocks for too long, hoping they will eventually recover, while quickly selling winning stocks to lock in profits. This behavior is MOST likely attributed to which combination of biases?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The disposition effect is the tendency to sell winners too early and hold losers too long. This is driven by loss aversion, where the pain of a loss is felt more strongly than the pleasure of an equivalent gain.  Therefore, the client is trying to avoid realizing losses (loss aversion) and prematurely capturing gains (disposition effect).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which biases directly relate to how investors treat winning and losing investments.
